Though it may seem clear that trucks must follow these signs in Michigan, many drivers wrongly view them as guidelines. In states like Ohio, where this driver is certified, the law does not exist, at least at the time of posting this video. The confusion stemmed from the 1988 law lacking a consequence clause, leaving no clear standard for officers to cite truckers. In 2021, the law was amended to classify the action as a civil violation, giving officers authority to stop and cite drivers. Despite the change, law makers and news reports through 2025 still indicate police cannot yet enforce the rule. Months after this very incident, representative William Brock explained why he is amending the law again in Michigan.

We've all faced the frustrations of driving on the freeways and especially when there's heavier vehicles that are just in front of you. His pathway to law enforcement and to the MSP was unique, given the fact that he was in his early 40s. How do you protect yourself from a threat that has no intention, no malice and no warning? A seizure behind the wheel turns a moving car into a 4,000 lb missile and no amount of training or awareness can stop what you can't see coming. That's the silent danger officers face every single day on the roadside. Not just criminals, not just recklessness, but the unpredictable. Some jurisdictions are still pretending it's 1995, but what if we stop pretending and what if we took the concept of officer safety beyond thoughts and prayers and turned it into policy and infrastructure? Well, that's exactly what Minnesota did. Instead of reacting to tragedy, they invested in prevention. They built a 911 system that's fully integrated with more than 1500 live traffic cameras, allowing dispatchers to see what's coming before it hits and alert troopers of real-time hazards fast.
